<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 I'm realizing the user wants to understand why a test harness is specifically needed for race conditions rather than relying on manual testing or standard unit tests—so I should explain how a harness creates the controlled conditions necessary to reliably reproduce and catch these timing-dependent bugs.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

Race conditions are uniquely hostile to ordinary debugging, and that's exactly why a harness exists. Here's the reasoning, grounded in what's happening in your repo.

## The core problem: races are non-deterministic

A race condition only manifests when two operations interleave in a specific order. In `tests/race.test.js`, the bug surfaces only if `/publish` reads state *after* `/draft` has started but *before* it has committed. In the wild, that window might be 5ms wide. Most of the time you click Publish, the save already finished — and the bug hides.

That means:
- **Manual testing is unreliable.** The README even tells you to click Publish "within about 200ms" — and you might still miss it.
- **A normal unit test isn't enough.** You can't just call `save()` then `publish()` synchronously; the bug *requires* concurrency to appear.

A harness is the tool that makes the bug **reproducible on demand**.

## What the harness actually does for you

Look at how `tests/race.test.js` constructs the scenario:

```38:46:tests/race.test.js
  const savePromise = agent
    .post('/draft')
    .send({ content: 'draft B' });

  // Immediately fire publish, before save B has had time to commit.
  const publishPromise = agent.post('/publish');

  // Let both finish in whatever order they finish.
  const [_, publishResponse] = await Promise.all([savePromise, publishPromise]);
```

And critically:

```17:19:tests/race.test.js
// Slow down the save commit to make the race reliable in test environments.
// Real production timing varies; for testing we control it.
process.env.SAVE_COMMIT_DELAY_MS = '300';
```

The harness gives you four things you can't get from manual testing:

1. **Control over timing.** It widens the race window (`SAVE_COMMIT_DELAY_MS=300`) so the bad interleaving happens *every* run instead of 1 in 100. This is the difference between a flaky bug and a deterministic one.
2. **Control over ordering.** It fires `save` without awaiting it, then fires `publish` immediately — guaranteeing publish lands while save is in flight. A human can't reliably hit that window.
3. **Isolation.** `/reset` between scenarios means each test starts from a known state, so failures are attributable to the code under test, not stale data.
4. **A machine-checkable assertion.** `assert.strictEqual(publishResponse.body.published, 'draft B', ...)` turns "did the bug happen?" into a binary signal that CI can gate on.

## Why this matters beyond just "passing the test"

A harness also serves three secondary purposes that are central to the assignment's debugging loop:

- **Proves the bug exists before you fix it.** Without a failing test, you can't tell whether you actually fixed anything or just got lucky on the next manual click.
- **Prevents regression.** Once the test is green, anyone who later refactors `server.js` and reintroduces the race will see CI fail on their PR (see `.github/workflows/test.yml`).
- **Forces you to articulate the bug precisely.** Writing the harness requires you to name the exact interleaving — "save in flight, publish reads stale state" — which is half the debugging work. The comments at the top of `tests/race.test.js` document this.

## The general principle

For race conditions, you can't rely on luck (manual clicking) or sequential reasoning (ordinary unit tests). You need a harness that:
- amplifies the race window so the bug is reproducible,
- pins down the ordering of concurrent operations, and
- asserts on the observable outcome.

That's why `harness/` exists as its own folder in the repo: building one is the actual deliverable of the assignment, not an afterthought.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

Isolation matters because **a race condition is a hypothesis about a specific interleaving**, and you can only confirm or refute that hypothesis if everything *except* the interleaving is held constant. Running the full app and hoping the bug fires is the opposite — it's an experiment with no controls.

Here's the breakdown, grounded in your repo.

## What "isolation" actually buys you

### 1. Eliminating confounding variables

If you reproduce the bug by running `npm start`, opening a browser, and clicking around, a failure could come from:

- network jitter between browser and server,
- the browser's own request scheduling,
- static asset loading in `app/static/index.html` competing for the event loop,
- whatever else your machine is doing,
- *or* the actual race in `/draft` and `/publish`.

When the bug fires, you don't know which of those caused it. When it *doesn't* fire, you don't know whether you fixed it or just got unlucky. The harness strips all of those out: same process, in-memory `supertest`, no network, no browser. The only variable left is the code under test.

### 2. Forcing the rare interleaving to happen every time

Look at how the production code is parameterized:

```40:46:app/server.js
// SAVE_COMMIT_DELAY_MS controls how long a /draft request takes to commit.
// In production this would represent database write latency, network latency,
// or any other delay between "request received" and "value updated."
//
// Set to 200ms by default to make the race condition reliably reproducible.
// Tests may override this via environment variable.
const SAVE_COMMIT_DELAY_MS = parseInt(process.env.SAVE_COMMIT_DELAY_MS || '200', 10);
```

In production, that "delay" is a real database write — maybe 5ms, maybe 50ms, maybe 500ms under load. The bug fires only when `/publish` arrives inside that window. If the window is 5ms wide, the bug fires roughly never on a developer's laptop.

The harness pins `SAVE_COMMIT_DELAY_MS = '300'` and fires `/publish` synchronously after `/draft` without awaiting. That converts a probabilistic bug into a deterministic one. "Hoping the bug fires" becomes "guaranteeing the bug fires."

### 3. Separating *bug present* from *bug observable*

A subtle point: even when a race is happening in production, users might not notice. They click Publish, see the wrong thing for a second, refresh, and it's correct now — chalked up to "weird internet." Without a controlled assertion you don't even know the bug exists.

The harness makes the bug **observable** by capturing the response body at the exact right moment and asserting on it:

```50:54:tests/race.test.js
  assert.strictEqual(
    publishResponse.body.published,
    'draft B',
    'publish returned stale data — the in-flight save was not reflected',
  );
```

That assertion is doing the work that a human eyeball can't do reliably.

### 4. Making "fixed" a falsifiable claim

Without isolation, "I fixed it" reduces to "I clicked around and didn't see the bug." That's not a fix — that's a vibe. With an isolated harness:

- **Before fix:** test fails, deterministically. Bug is proven.
- **After fix:** test passes, deterministically. Fix is proven.
- **Six months from now:** if someone refactors `app/server.js` and reintroduces the race, CI catches it on their PR.

The fix in `app/server.js` (the `Promise.allSettled([...pendingDraftCommits])` await on lines 109–116) is meaningful *because* there's an isolated test that can distinguish "this code waits for pending commits" from "this code happens to work on my machine today."

### 5. Letting you reset the world between scenarios

```28:32:tests/race.test.js
  // Reset shared state between test runs.
  await agent.post('/reset').expect(200);
```

The full app accumulates state — `currentDraft`, `publishedDraft`, in-flight timers. If your "test" is just clicking around, scenario N's state contaminates scenario N+1. The third test in `race.test.js` is precisely about this: it verifies that `/reset` cancels in-flight commits so they don't bleed into the next scenario. That test is only meaningful because the harness controls the boundaries between scenarios.

## The general principle

Running the full app and hoping is **observation without control**. A harness is an **experiment** in the scientific sense: hold every other variable fixed, manipulate the one variable you care about (the timing/ordering between save and publish), and check whether the outcome matches your hypothesis.

Race conditions specifically demand this discipline because:

- the bug is rare under normal conditions,
- the bug is invisible without precise observation,
- the "fix" can easily be wrong-but-lucky,
- and the bug can be reintroduced silently by future changes.

Isolation isn't a nice-to-have for race-condition debugging — it's the only way to make claims that aren't just anecdotes.
